Facts.text: Pope Celestine V resigns, after just over 5 months in office
Saint Celestine V abdicates the papacy after only five months; Celestine hoped to return to his previous life as an ascetic hermit.
The greatest traveller of his age,Ibn Battutaof Tangier completes his work "The Travels" detailing his 29 years spent travelling the Islamic world and beyond, including to India, China and Indonesia
John III succeeds his father as King of Portugal and the Algarves, at age 19; reigns for  36 years, until his death in 1557
Sweden and Denmark signs Peace of Stettin
Spanish army beats Geuzen fleet (confederacy of Calvinist Dutch nobles) under admiral Lumey
EmperorFerdinand IIdelegates 1st anti-Reformation decree
The Massachusetts Bay Colony organizes three militia regiments to defend the colony against the Pequot Indians. Recognized today as the founding of the United States National Guard.
English Civil War: The Battle of Alton takes place in Hampshire.
Prussia joins Austria and Russia in signing the Treaty of Berlin, a secret treaty regarding succession to the Polish throne
Britain and Russia sign trade agreement
William IV Prince of Orange tests his mothers potatoes
First music store in America opens (Philadelphia)
Dartmouth College in New Hampshire receives its charter
Patent for a dry dock issued to John Adamson, Boston
Battle of Alleghany Summit, West Virginia
Battle of Fredericksburg, Virginia (Marye's Heights)
Battle of Fort McAllister, Georgia
First federal fish hatching steamer launched (Wilmington, Delaware)
Belgium rules on women and child labor law
George Gunn scores 119 on Test debut v Australia SCG
Avalanche kills 10,000 Austrian and Italian troops in 24 hrs in Tyrol
Esme Stuart Lennox Robinsons premieres in Dublin
French chief of staff Joffre replaced by Nivelle
US army of occupation crosses Rhine, enters Germany
Francis G. Pease's interferometer at Mount Wilson Observatory is the first to measure the diameter of a star - the Betelgeuse
Hans Riegel founds sweet company HARIBO (HAns RIegel BOnn) in Bonn, Germany
League of Nations establishes the International Court of Justice in The Hague
Netherland breaks contact with Kingdom of Serbia, Croatia and Slavia
KOA-AM in Denver CO begins radio transmissions
Clip-on tie designed
French government of André Tardieu falls for the second time
George Sisler's career ends when Boston Braves release him
Theodore Steeg forms French government
Mark Hellinger Theater (Warner Bros) opens at 237 W 51st St NYC
Japanese troops conquer and plunder Nanjing (Nanjing Massacre)
Los Angeles, California freezes at 28°F
Battle of the River Plate - 3 British cruisers vs German pocket battleship Graf Spee
German occupiers forbid National Front and Netherland Union
Lawine battlers destroy Haaraz, Peru; about 3,000 die
Seyss-Inquart allows Dutch Nazi Anton Mussert to call himself Leader
150 US Marauders bomb Schiphol
Japanese kamikaze crashes into US cruiser Nashville, kills 138
Norman Krasna's "Dear Ruth" premieres in NYC
Maine Turnpike opens to traffic
AL votes down proposal to revive spitball
Knesset votes to transfer Israel's capital to Jerusalem
NFL Dallas Texans (former NY Yanks) play final game, losing 41-6 to the Lions in Detroit; finish with record of 1-11
KOAM TV channel 7 in Pittsburg-Joplin, KS (CBS) begins broadcasting
Archbishop Makarios elected 1st president of Cyprus, receiving two thirds of the vote
Laos General Fumi Nosavang occupies Vientiane
Relay 1 communication satellite launched
Capital Records signs right of first refusal agreement with The Beatles
Algerian president Boumedienne visits Moscow
1st battle for Bijlmer flats, Amsterdam
Test debut ofClive Lloyd, v India Bombay, 82 & 78
US performs nuclear test at Nevada Test Site
Greek King Constantine II attempts a counter-coup against the military junta; after the failure, Constantine flees to Italy
San Diego, CA records snow at a zero elevation after temperatures plunge 19 degrees (F) in eight hours.
United Soccer Association & National Pro Soccer League merge into NASL
Brazilian President Da Costa e Silva issues Ato Institucional Número Cinco degree
John Sinclair (sentence: 10 yrs, sold 2 marijuana joints) is freed
MPLA/FNLA accord about combat against Portuguese Libya
World Football League grants 1st franchise (Detroit)
Malta becomes a republic
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
West Indian cricket opener Roy Fredericks smashes 169 in 2nd Test win v Australia in Perth; hundred in 71 balls
Golden Gate Bridge District starts ferry service to Larkspur
Longest non-stop passenger airflight (Sydney to San Francisco 13h 14m)
14 University of Evansville basketball players die in plane crash
CBS' premiere of "Lovey: A Circle of Children Part II", sequel to "A Circle of Children", which is also based on Mary Mac Cracken
Saudi Arabia raises marker crude price to $24 per barrel
Strikes against price increases in Gdańsk, Poland
Poland's authoritarian communist government declares martial law and arrests Solidarity activists in a failed attempt to crush the opposition
Polish communist government jails former communist party leader Edward Gierek
Earthquake hits Northern Yemen; 2,000 die
9,655 see highest-scoring NBA game: Detroit 186, Denver 184 (3 OT)
Islander's Butch Goring scorings 4 goals against Oilers
KYA-AM in San Francisco CA changes call letters to KOIT
Martha Layne Collins inaugurated as Kentucky's 1st female governor
Artificial heart recipient William Schroeder suffers 1st stroke
Test debut of cricketers Merv Hughes, Geoff Marsh and Bruce Reid (v India)
Belgium Christian Democrats (CVP) loses parliamentary election
Browns set club record for most points scored in a quarter, 28
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
3 men end 29-hr all-466-station subway ride in NYC
The last issue of Gnistan (The Spark), the organ of the Solidaritetspartiet, is published in Sweden
Walter Davis of the Denver Nuggets ends NBA free throw streak of 53 games
Heavy earthquake strikes Sicily, 18 die
US Secretary of State Baker questions Iraq's seriousness about Middle East peace
Both North and South Korea sign an accord calling for reconciliation
NY assembly speaker Mel Miller is convicted of federal mail fraud
Ricky Pierce (Seattle) ends NBA free throw streak of 75 games
FCC fines Infinity Broadcasting $600,000
"I Swear" single released by John Michael Montgomery (Billboard Song of the Year 1994)
Deadline for Israeli troop withdrawal from Gaza, they don't
Dow Jones Industrial Average hits record 3764.43
Fire in textile factory in Fuzjou China, 60 killed
Space shuttle STS-61 (Endeavour 5) lands after 10 days and 19 hours in space
American Eagle commuter plane crashes in NC, killing 15
US Federal Court votes that Cable companies must carry local stations
63rd Heisman Trophy Award: Charles Woodson, Michigan (CB)
65th Heisman Trophy Award: Ron Dayne, Wisconsin (RB)
The "Texas 7" escape from the John Connally Unit near Kenedy, Texas later going on a crime spree and killing police officer Aubrey Hawkins
Indian Parliament Sansad attacked by terrorists. 15 people killed, including all terrorists
The European Union announces that Cyprus, the Czech Republic, Estonia, Hungary, Latvia, Lithuania, Malta, Poland, Slovakia and Slovenia will become members from May 1, 2004
69th Heisman Trophy Award: Jason White, Oklahoma (QB)
Australian mechanic Bradley John Murdoch is found guilty of the murder of Peter Falconio and sentenced to life in prison
The Baiji, or Chinese River Dolphin, announced as extinct.

The Mitchell Report is publicly released listing the names of 89 Major League Baseball players that have presumably used anabolic steroids and human growth hormones. Notable players named includeRoger Clemensand Miguel Tejada.
76th Heisman Trophy Award: Cam Newton, Auburn (QB)
A car bombing in Qatana, outside Damascus, kills 16 people and injures 12
Japan scrambles fighter jets in response to a Chinese plane in the Senkaku Islands dispute
Mosconi Cup nine-ball pool, London, England: Team Europe win for 3rd consecutive year beating US, 11-9; MVP: Chris Melling (England)
80th Heisman Trophy Award: Marcus Mariota, Oregon (QB)
A civil rights protest march in Washington, D.C. takes place, against the police killing of unarmed black men
Divisions emerge between rich and poor nations at the UN climate change talks in Peru
Fatou Bensouda, the chief prosecutor of the International Criminal Court, criticizes UN Security Council for its lack of action over war crimes in the Darfur region of Sudan
The British Government announces it will send hundreds of troops to Iraq, to train Iraqi and Kurdish forces in the conflict with Islamic State
Two US soldiers killed in a Taliban bombing of a NATO convoy in Kabul
UN claims 82 civilians have been summary executed in Aleppo by pro-government forces
Democratic Republic of Congo jails for life 11 militiamen for raping 40 children, including a local MP
Online dictionary Merriam-Webster's most searched word of the year is Feminism
Prehistoric bones of a penguin as tall as a human (1.77m) found on Otago beach, New Zealand, reported in "Nature Communications"
Scientists begin checking interstellar asteroid Oumuamua for signs of alien technology through radio signals (later studies conclude it a piece of sliced off planet)
Actress Eliza Dushku revealed received $9.5 million settlement from CBS after sexual harassment on set of "Bull" from actor Michael Weatherley
Apple announces new $1 billion campus in Austin, Texas and expansion in other US cities
Cease-fire in port city of Hudaydah, Yemen, by Houthi rebels and Saudi-led coalition announced by the UN
German basketball forward Dirk Nowitzki takes the court for his record 21st NBA season with the Dallas Mavericks, surpassing Kobe Bryant's 20 seasons with the LA Lakers
Passenger train collides with maintenance locomotive in Ankara, Turkey, killing at least 9 and injuring 47
US Senate passes non-binding "war powers resolution" against long-term ally Saudi Arabia
Germany announces a strict lockdown till Jan 10 after looser restrictions failed to prevent COVID-19 numbers surging
1 in a 100 Americans aged over 65 has now died of COVID-19, or 75% of total deaths, according to the CDC
First nuclear fusion reaction in a laboratory setting, replicating the same energy that powers the sun - announced by scientists at Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory, California
New Zealand passes world's first tobacco ban, outlawing smoking for those born after 2009
Sam Bankman-Fried, founder of the collapsed cryptocurrency exchange FTX, is arrested in the Bahamas after US files criminal charges against him, including wire fraud and money laundering
COP28 in Dubai ends with agreement from 190 counties to begin “transitioning away from fossil fuels”, a compromise but still the first to directly address an end to fossil fuels
Researchers pinpoint possible cause of extreme morning sickness in pregnancy - low levels of the hormone GDF15
US Center for Disease Control confirms the first severe case of a person with bird flu in Louisiana, out of more than 60 in 2024
Rebels holed up inside Kenilworth Castle surrender after 172 days toHenry III, ending one of the longest sieges in medieval English history
Isabella Icrowns herself Queen of Castile and León
Council of Trent(19th ecumenical council) opened by Pope Paul III
Dutch explorerAbel Tasmansights the South Island of present day New Zealand; initially he calls it Staten Landt and changes it a year later to Nieuw Zeeland
Jean Racine's tragic play "Britannicus" premieres in Paris
Paul Revereand Wentworth Cheswell ride to warn Portsmouth of the approach of British warships
Italian opera composerGioachino Rossiniarrives in London
War of the Triple Alliance: Paraguay officially declares war on Brazil, invades the next day
First complete performance ofGustav Mahler's 2nd Symphony ("Resurrection"), in Berlin, Germany, conducted by the composer
Battle of Nooitgedacht: Boer GeneralsKoos de la ReyandJan Smutsdefeat a British brigade under the command of Major General R. Clements

British and German ships bombard Venezuelan forts after President Castro refuses to comply with ultimatum demanding damages caused during his takeover of the government in 1899; Castro asks US PresidentTheodore Rooseveltto arbitrate
German chancellorBernhard von Bülowdisbands the Parliament
German EmperorWilhelm IIvisits Amsterdam
British  Foreign SecretaryEdward Greyproposes that southern Albania be divided between Greece and Albania with compensation to Greece in the Aegean islands
Charles Ebbetsproposes putting numbers on players' sleeves or caps
George Gershwin's "An American In Paris" symphonic poem premieres at Carnegie Hall, NYC, performed by the New York Philharmonic conducted by Walter Damrosch
Léon Blumagain elected French Prime Minister
James Deanbegins his career with an appearance in a Pepsi commercial
Elsa Schiaparelli's House of Schiaparelli at Place Vendôme in Paris closes
"Anastasia" comeback film forIngrid Bergmanis released in the US, role wins Bergman Academy Award for Best Actress.
Dodgers tradeJackie Robinsonto Giants for pitcher Dick Littlefield & $35,000. Robinson will retire rather than be traded.
"Peyton Place" film based on the novel by Grace Metalious, directed by Mark Robson and starringLana Turnerand Hope Lange is released
Ballon d'Or: Barcelona midfielderLuis Suárezis named best football player in Europe ahead of Real Madrid's Hungarian forward Ferenc Puskás and Hamburg striker Uwe Seeler
"The Young Ones" directed by Sidney J. Furie, starringCliff Richard, Robert Morley and Carole Gray premieres in London
In El Paso, Texas,LBJ& Mexican President Gustavo Diaz Ordaz set off an explosion diverting Rio Grande, to reshape US-Mexico border
MLB managerBilly Martinfired by the Minnesota Twins
Greg Chappellscores 108 on Test debut v England at the WACA
Neil Simon's play "Gingerbread Lady" starring Maureen Stapleton (Tony Award Best Actress), premieres on Broadway in NYC
1st time "Saturday Night Live" uses a time delay,Richard Pryorhosts
Australian Federal Election - Liberals with coalition underMalcolm Fraserwin largest ever parliamentary majority
Asylum Records releases "Don Juan's Reckless Daughter", Canadian singer-songwriterJoni Mitchell's 9th studio album;
Yasser Arafataddresses UN in Geneva
"Driving Miss Daisy" directed by Bruce Beresford and starringMorgan FreemanandJessica Tandyis released (Best Picture 1990)
South African PresidentF. W. de Klerkmeets withNelson Mandelato discuss the end of apartheid
"The Hits" 2nd compilation album byGarth Brooksis released (Billboard Album of the Year 1995)
Christopher Reevereleased from physical rehab center after his fall from his horse in a riding competition
Free agentRoger Clemenssigns with Toronto Blue Jays
Kofi Annanis elected Secretary-General of the United Nations
American Vice PresidentAl Goredelivers his concession speech effectively ending his hopes of becoming the 43rd President of the United States.
"A Beautiful Mind" based on the bio by Sylvia Nasar, directed by Ron Howard and starringRussell Crowepremieres in Los Angeles (Best Picture 2002)
Former Iraqi PresidentSaddam Husseinis captured near his home town of Tikrit, during Operation Red Dawn by US forces
Ballon d'Or: Milan's Ukrainian striker Andriy Shevchenko is named best football player in Europe ahead of Deco (Porto/FC Barcelona) and Barcelona midfielderRonaldinho

Former Chilean dictator, GeneralAugusto Pinochetis put under house arrest, after being sued under accusations over 9 kidnapping actions and manslaughter. The house arrest is lifted the same day on appeal.
"Beyoncé" 5th studio album by Beyoncé is released
81st Heisman Trophy Award:Derrick Henry, Alabama (RB)
IrishmanConor McGregorknocks out Brazilian Jose Aldo in 13 seconds to win his 1st featherweight title in Las Vegas
ActressSalma Hayekaccuses movie mogulHarvey Weinsteinof sexual harassment and threatening to kill her
Prime MinisterTheresa May's UK Conservative government loses key vote in Parliament for an amendment to allow MPs to vote on Brexit
Michael Bay's "6 Underground" is released on Netflix

British Mercedes driverLewis Hamiltonfinishes 3rd in season-ending Abu Dhabi GP to claim record equalling 7th F1 World Drivers C'ship by 124 points from teammate Valtteri Bottas; Mercedes' 7th straight Constructors title
Elon Musk, CEO ofSpaceXand Tesla, named Time's Person of the Year
First COVID-19 Omicron death in the UK reported by PMBoris Johnson
Marvel superhero film "Spider-Man: No Way Home" starringTom Holland,Zendaya, andBenedict Cumberbatch, directed by Jon Watts, premieres in Los Angeles
US PresidentJoe Bidensigns the Respect for Marriage Act into law, mandating federal recognition of same-sex marriages in ceremony at the White House
Washington Capitals left wingAlex Ovechkinscores a hat trick in 7-3 away win over Chicago Blackhawks to join Wayne Gretzky and Gordie Howe as the only players in NHL history to score 800 goals
British rock bandColdplayrelease a music video for their song "All My Love" featuring American entertainerDick Van Dyke, on his 99th birthday
Francis Drakesets sail from England on an epic three year circumnavigation of the world aboard the 'Pelican' (later renamed the 'Golden Hind')
Gideon Hausner in Jerusalem demands death penalty forAdolf Eichmann

Erik XIV, King of Sweden (1560-69), born in Stockholm (d. 1577)

Gustav Luders, German-American conductor and stage musical composer (The Prince of Pilsen), born in Bremen, Germany (d. 1913)
Kristian Birkeland, Norwegian explorer and scientist (nominated 7 times for Nobel Prize), born in Oslo, Norway (d. 1917)
Edward LeSaint, American actor and director (Modern Times, The Dumb Messenger), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1940)
Emily Carr, Canadian painter, born in Victoria, British Columbia, Canada (d. 1945)
Herman Theodoor Colenbrander, Dutch historian, born in Drachten, Netherlands (d. 1945)

Ludwig Curtius, German archaeologist (Die antike Kunst), born in Augsburg, Germany (d. 1954)
Mykola Leontovych, Ukrainian composer (Carol of the Bells), born in Selevyntsi, Podolia, Russian Empire (d. 1921)

Lambertus Johannes van Apeldoorn, Dutch lawyer (martial law), born in IJlst, Netherlands (d. 1979)
Alvin York, American World War I soldier who led a famed attack on a German machine gun nest (Medal of Honor), born in Pall Mall, Tennessee (d. 1964)
George Pólya, Hungarian mathematician, born in Budapest, Austria-Hungary (d. 1985)

Drew Pearson, American newscaster (Drew Pearson), born in Evanston, Illinois (d. 1969)
Daniel Lazarus, French composer, born in Paris, France (d. 1964)

Ingemar Liljefors, Swedish composer, born in Gothenburg, Sweden (d. 1981)
Laurens Jan van der Post, South African-born writer, soldier, explorer and conservationist, born in Philippolis (d. 1996)

Victor Babin, Russian-American concert pianist (Vronsky & Babin), composer, and educator (Cleveland Institute, 1961-72), born in Moscow, Russia (d. 1972)
Charles Alfred Coulson, British chemist (theoretical), born in Dudley, England (d. 1974)
Lillian Roth [Rutstein], American singer and actress (Animal Crackers), born in Boston, Massachusetts (d. 1980)
Buzz Borries, American College Football Hall of Fame halfback (Navy 1932–34), born in Louisville, Kentucky (d. 1969)
Trygve Haavelmo, Norwegian economist and 1989 Nobel laureate for his contributions to econometrics, born in Skedsmo, Norway (d. 1999)
Jimmy Carroll, American pianist (Most Important People), born in New York City (d. 1972)
John Wyndham Pope-Hennessy, English art historian and director of British Museum (1974-76), born in London (d. 1994)
Alan Bullock, British historian and author (Hitler: A Study in Tyranny), born in Trowbridge, Wiltshire, England (d. 2004)
George "Tiger" Haynes, American jazz guitarist (The Three Flames), and stage and screen actor (The Wiz; In the Heat of the Night), born in Frederiksted, St. Croix (d. 1994)

Balthazar Johannes Vorster, Prime Minister of South Africa (1966-77), born in Uitenhage, Eastern Cape, South Africa (d. 1983)
Curd Jürgens [Curt Jurgens], German-born Austrian actor (The Enemy Below; The Longest Day), born in Solln, Munich, Bavaria, Germany (d. 1982)
Ross MacDonald [Kenneth Millar], American-Canadian detective novelist (Goodbye Look), born in Los Gatos, California (d. 1983)
Yitzhak Zuckerman [Itzhak Zuckerman], Jewish resistance hero in World War II and one of the few survivors of the Warsaw Ghetto Uprising, born in Vilnius, Lithuania (d. 1981)

Dave Street [Patrick Devlin], American actor and singer (Moonrise; I Surrender Dear; Broadway Open House), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 1971)
John Hart, American actor (The Lone Ranger, The Adventures of Captain Africa), born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2009)
Bill Vukovich, Americanauto racer(Indianapolis 500, 1953-4), born in Fresno, California (d. 1955)
Hans-Joachim Marseille, German flying ace of World War II, born in Berlin, Weimar Republic (d. 1942)
Donald "Don" Taylor, American director and actor (Father's Little Dividend; Escape from the Planet of the Apes), born in Freeport, Pennsylvania (d. 1998)
Frits Noske, Dutch musicologist (Signifier & the Signified), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 1993)
Kaysone Phomvihane, President of Laos (Pathet Lao) (1991-92), born in Na Seng village, Khanthabouli district (d. 1992)
Jackie Pung, American golfer (5 LPGA titles), born in Honolulu, Hawaii (d. 2017)
José Sarria, American political activist, drag queen and 1st openly gay candidate for public office in the US (1961), born in San Francisco, California (d. 2013)
Antoni Tapies, Spanish painter, sculptor and theorist (Dau al Set), born in Barcelona, Spain (d. 2012)
Frankie Fraser, English gangster who spent 42 years in jail, born in London, England (d. 2014)
Fred van der Spek, Dutch 2nd chamber member (PSP), born in The Hague, Netherlands (d. 2017)
Albert Paulsen, Ecuadorian-American actor (One Day in the Life of Ivan Denisovich, Doctors' Hospital), born in Guayaquil, Ecuador (d. 2004)

Geneviève Page [Bonjean], French actress (Belle de Jour; The Day and the Hour; Three Rooms in Manhattan), born in Paris, France (d. 2025)[1]

W. Gordon Smith, Scottish playwright, born in Edinburgh, Scotland (d. 1996)
Robert Prosky, Polish-American actor and comedian (Christine, Thief), born in Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (d. 2008)
Lou Adler, American music executive, director, producer, and co-owner of the famous Roxy Theatre, born in Chicago, Illinois
Richard Darryl Zanuck, American Academy Award-winning film producer, born in Los Angeles, California (d. 2012)
Arthur Summons, Australian rugby union fly-half (10 Tests; Gordon RFC) and rugby league five-eighth (9 Tests; Western Suburbs RLFC), born in Sydney, Australia (d. 2020)
Joe Christopher, American MLB baseball outfielder, 1959-1966 (Pittsburgh Pirates, New York Mets, Boston Red Sox), born in Frederiksted, U.S. Virgin Islands (d. 2023)
Lindy McDaniel, American baseball pitcher (MLB All Star 1960; NL saves leader 1959, 60, 63), born in Hollis, Oklahoma
Tom Wakefield, British writer, born in Staffordshire, England (d. 1996)
Aga Khan IV [Karim Aga Khan,] Imam of Nizari-Ismaili and philanthropist, born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 2025)[1]
J. C. Martin, American baseball player, born in Axton, Virginia
Rob Houwer, Dutch director and producer and actor (OK, Professor Columbus), born in The Hague, Netherlands
Alvin Curran, American composer (Inner Cities; I Dreamt John Cage Yodeling at the Zurich Hauptbahnhof), born in Providence, Rhode Island
Heino [Heinz Georg Kramm], German schlager and traditional volksmusik singer, born in Düsseldorf-Oberbilk, Germany
Tom Shaw, American golfer (The Tradition 1993), born in Wichita, Kansas
Tony Gomez, British rock keyboardist (The Foundations - "Baby Now That I've Found You"; "Build Me Up Buttercup"), born in Colombo, Ceylon (now Sri Lanka) (d. 2015)
Edith Clever, German actress (Parsifal, L'Adolescente), born in Wuppertal, Germany
Steve Harmon, American actor (Ens Pulver-Mr Roberts), born in Brooklyn, New York
Anouska Hempel, British actress (Tiffany Jones), born in Wellington, New Zealand
Elizabeth Pochoda, American journalist who helped revitalize Vanity Fair magazine, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2025)
John Davidson, American TV host (Hollywood Squares, That's Incredible), born in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania
Robert Covington, American drummer and singer, born in Yazoo City, Mississippi (d. 1996)

(Margaret) "Marti" Webb, British actress and singer (Evita; Tell Me on a Sunday), born in Hampstead, London
